sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
A nonhealing foot ulcer.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports having a two-month history of a nonhealing foot ulcer, which recently became red and began draining. He states he acquired the ulcer while walking barefoot on the beach. He previously received antibiotics but did not pick up the medication. He does not recall the exact name of the medication. He reports occasional throbbing pain, but no specific pain, and expresses concern about the drainage and redness. He has not experienced this before. The patient's diabetes is poorly controlled, as he does not regularly check his blood sugar levels. His last A1c reading was approximately ten.
Findings
A one by two inch circular wound is present on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth metatarsal head (5th MT). The exam reveals redness, drainage, and edema around the wound. There is no evidence of necrosis, odor, or bony exposure. Vital signs are normal, with no fever. An x-ray of the right foot revealed no evidence of osteomyelitis. A surgical shoe may be considered.
Assessment
The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with signs of infection.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: Order an ankle brachial index (ABI) to assess blood supply to the foot. Perform debridement of the ulcer. Prescribe clindamycin 400 mg twice daily for seven days to treat the infection. Obtain a surgical shoe for the patient to wear while the ulcer heals. Schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to assess healing and potentially perform further debridement. Advise the patient to call the office if any concerns arise or if he experiences a fever.
